title: SACRAMENTO, CA – In an effort to reduce housing costs, encourage sustainable growth, and support California’s climate goals, Senator Josh Becker (D-Menlo Park) has introduced SB 35

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "Senator Josh Becker introduced SB 358, legislation aimed at incentivizing transit-oriented development by reducing vehicular traffic impact fees for housing near public transit.",
    "Impact fees can add nearly 20% to the cost of construction, making new housing more expensive to build and rent.",
    "SB 358 helps lower housing costs and makes transit-friendly housing more financially feasible.",
    "The Sheetz v. County of El Dorado opinion affirmed that monetary exactions for building permits are subject to the Nollan/Dolan test of 'essential nexus' and 'rough proportionality.'",
    "New developments must mitigate their impacts and fees must reflect the actual impacts of the project.",
    "SB 358 ensures that infill housing developments near transit are charged fees that reflect the location efficiency of these sites and their reduced vehicle trips.",
    "SB 358 aligns traffic mitigation fees with actual traffic impact and rewards cities and developers for building homes that support walking, biking, and transit.",
    "People who live in housing developments near transit tend to walk more, take transit more, and drive less.",
    "High development fees increase overall housing costs, making new homes less affordable and suppressing housing supply.",
    "Research shows that high development fees discourage transit-oriented development, despite California's commitment to reducing vehicle emissions.",
    "SB 358 requires local agencies to reduce vehicular traffic impact fees by at least 50% for developments within designated transit priority areas.",
    "Reducing traffic impact fees encourages housing development near public transit, reducing car dependency and emissions.",
    "SB 358 strengthens the fiscal health of local governments by increasing property tax revenues through incentivizing new development."
  ]
}
```
